title.en: Pausing or blocking work for a compliance concern
title.es: Pausar o bloquear trabajo por una preocupación de cumplimiento

question.en: Tell me about a time you paused or blocked work because of a compliance, legal, or policy concern. How did you handle the tension between speed and correctness?
question.es: Cuéntame sobre una ocasión en la que pausaste o bloqueaste trabajo debido a una preocupación de cumplimiento, legal o de política. ¿Cómo manejaste la tensión entre velocidad y corrección?

must_include: the specific compliance, legal, or policy dimension that triggered the pause — named explicitly (e.g., 'this feature would collect biometric data without explicit opt-in, which triggered BIPA obligations' or 'the vendor contract didn't cover the new data-sharing use case under our data governance policy')
how the candidate identified the concern — was it noticed during review, flagged by a partner team, surfaced through a process, or the candidate's own reading of a policy or regulation
the concrete cost of pausing — to the team, timeline, or stakeholders — and how the candidate communicated and owned that cost rather than passing it to the compliance concern as an abstraction

the distinction between following policy (weak signal: 'the rules say we have to stop') and articulating the underlying reason (strong signal: 'I paused because if we ship without resolving this, we expose customers to X and the company to Y') — which did the candidate use?

how the candidate balanced the speed-correctness tension in practice — not just that they held for correctness, but the specific steps taken to reduce the delay while not compromising the compliance requirement
must_avoid: claiming the compliance concern was obvious or that anyone reasonable would have paused — DTRT requires naming the pressure to keep moving and describing how the candidate held against it

using the compliance concern as a shield without taking ownership of the impact — 'Legal told us we had to stop' is not DTRT, it is deferring to authority; the candidate must name their own role in raising or holding the concern
vague 'I flagged the issue' framing with no specifics on what the concern was, how it was escalated, or what resolution looked like
